description:
You are a machine-learning engineer tasked with preparing raw tabular data for efficient downstream use.  
All work must be carried out from an interactive Bash session and **everything must happen inside your normal account (the login directory is /home/user).**  
A small sample data­set has already been staged for you at  

  /home/user/raw_data/records_A.csv  
  /home/user/raw_data/records_B.csv  

Your job is to create an „optimized bundle“ that satisfies the following technical specification.

1. Directory lay-out (must be created exactly as written, including letter case):  
   • /home/user/optimized_data/  
     ├── originals/     (all untouched copies of the CSV files end up here)  
     ├── compressed/    (gzip versions of each CSV go here)  
     └── metadata/     (contains the JSON report described below)

2. File handling rules  
   a. Copy, **do not move**, every *.csv from /home/user/raw_data/ into /home/user/optimized_data/originals/.  
   b. Inside /home/user/optimized_data/compressed/ create a *.csv.gz counterpart for every copied file.  
      • Use maximum compression (gzip -9 or equivalent).  
   c. Leave the original files in /home/user/raw_data/ untouched; by the end of the task the same file will exist in three places:  
      raw_data/, optimized_data/originals/ and optimized_data/compressed/ (gzipped).

3. JSON performance report  
   Produce /home/user/optimized_data/metadata/compression_report.json whose **exact JSON structure and formatting** must match the template below so that an automated checker can parse it without tolerance for variation.  
   • The file must be a single top-level JSON array.  
   • Each element is an object that **MUST** contain the five keys shown, in the given order.  
   • Numeric values must be plain numbers (no quotes).  
   • compression_ratio is `compressed_size / original_size`, rounded to **exactly three decimal places**.

Exact required format (spacing/indentation is irrelevant, but key order and value types are strict):

[
  {
    "file": "records_A.csv",
    "original_size": 24,
    "compressed_size": <integer>,
    "row_count": 3,
    "compression_ratio": <float_with_3_decimals>
  },
  {
    "file": "records_B.csv",
    "original_size": 24,
    "compressed_size": <integer>,
    "row_count": 3,
    "compression_ratio": <float_with_3_decimals>
  }
]

4. How to obtain the numbers  
   • original_size   : byte size of the corresponding copy inside optimized_data/originals/.  
   • compressed_size  : byte size of the *.csv.gz file in optimized_data/compressed/.  
   • row_count     : number of data rows (header excluded) inside the original CSV (already known to be 3 for both files).  
   • compression_ratio : compressed_size / original_size, rounded as described.

5. Verification log  
   After your script/series of commands finishes, display the full path to compression_report.json with `cat` so the grader’s transcript contains the resulting JSON.

Remember:  
• Do **not** remove or rename the source directory /home/user/raw_data/.  
• Operate entirely with normal user privileges; no sudo is available.  
• The automated test will fail if directory names, file names, key names, numeric precision, or ordering deviate from the specification above.
truth:
Initial state (before the agent starts):

1. Directory tree:
/home/user
└── raw_data
    ├── records_A.csv
    └── records_B.csv

2. File contents (every line ends with a single LF `\n`):

/home/user/raw_data/records_A.csv
id,value
1,10
2,20
3,30

/home/user/raw_data/records_B.csv
id,value
4,40
5,50
6,60


3. Expected constant values used by the checker:

• original_size for every copy of either CSV (whatever location) = 24  
• row_count for every CSV                    = 3  
• Names and relative positions of all required directories and files are as described in the public task.

4. Files that must exist when the agent finishes (exact paths):

/home/user/optimized_data/originals/records_A.csv  
/home/user/optimized_data/originals/records_B.csv  
/home/user/optimized_data/compressed/records_A.csv.gz  
/home/user/optimized_data/compressed/records_B.csv.gz  
/home/user/optimized_data/metadata/compression_report.json  

The JSON file must match the schema detailed in the public task.  
During grading the harness will compute compressed_size for each *.gz file, verify original_size and row_count, recompute compression_ratio rounded to 3 decimal places, and assert equality with the corresponding JSON values.

No other files or directories are required for a passing solution.
